case_name: Lalita Kumari v. Govt. of Uttar Pradesh
key_held: Registration of FIR is mandatory under CrPC 154 if the information discloses commission of a cognizable offence. No preliminary enquiry unless the case falls within the exceptions carved out.
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)
notes: Constitution Bench. Foundation for mandatory FIR. BNSS 173 largely codifies this principle.

case_name: Satvinder Kaur v. State (Govt. of NCT of Delhi)
key_held: Police cannot refuse to register FIR on the ground of lack of territorial jurisdiction. A woman has the right to lodge complaint at any police station.
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)
notes: Precursor to Zero FIR doctrine. Widely relied upon by High Courts and codified in BNSS Sec 173(1).

case_name: State of Haryana v. Bhajan Lal
key_held: The word 'information' in Section 154 is not qualified by 'reasonable' or 'credible'. Police has no discretion to refuse registration if cognizable offence is disclosed.
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)
notes: Classic on FIR registration. Prevents police from screening cases.

case_name: Mukesh v. State (NCT of Delhi) (Nirbhaya)
key_held: Prompt registration of FIR is crucial to preserve evidence and ensure fair trial. Object is to obtain early information about accused, nature of incident and witnesses.
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)
notes: Nirbhaya case. Emphasized prompt FIR in serious crimes.

case_name: Nand Lal v. State of Chhattisgarh
key_held: Immediate FIR is crucial especially in group clashes/fights between parties at loggerheads to remove suspicion of over-implication.
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)
notes: Recent reaffirmation of timely FIR importance.

case_name: State of U.P. v. Raghuvir
key_held: Unexplained delay in lodging FIR is viewed with suspicion as it increases possibility of concoction or embellishment.
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)

case_name: Satpal v. State of Haryana
key_held: FIR is not an encyclopedia and does not require every single detail of the occurrence.
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)

case_name: Motiram Padu Joshi v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Omission of names of assailants or witnesses in FIR is not automatically fatal, especially if FIR is lodged without delay.
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)

case_name: Soma Bhai v. State of Gujarat
key_held: A telephonic message can constitute an FIR provided it discloses commission of a cognizable offence.
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)
notes: Early jurisprudence on what constitutes FIR.

case_name: Anurag Bhatnagar v. State (NCT of Delhi)
key_held: Magistrate should ordinarily not entertain direct application under Section 156(3) unless complainant has exhausted police remedies (SHO then SP).
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)
notes: 2025 ruling on hierarchy of remedies. Impacts BNSS applications.

case_name: State of Orissa v. Sharat Chandra Sahu
key_held: Police investigating a cognizable offence are not barred from investigating any non-cognizable offence arising from the same facts.
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)

case_name: Mukesh Singh v. State (NCT of Delhi)
key_held: Compelling accused to attend Test Identification Parade (TIP) does not violate Article 20(3).
chapter_name: FIR Registration & Zero FIR (BNSS Sec 173 / CrPC 154)

case_name: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar
key_held: Police must not arrest routinely in offences punishable with less than 7 years (esp. 498A). Must record reasons for arrest necessity per CrPC 41. Notice u/s 41A preferred.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)
notes: Landmark on arrest as exception. Directly influenced BNSS Section 35.

case_name: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
key_held: 11 mandatory guidelines for arrest and detention: inform grounds of arrest, right to lawyer, medical examination, family intimation, etc. to prevent custodial torture.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)
notes: Constitutional cornerstone. Enforced nationwide. BNSS incorporates many.

case_name: Joginder Kumar v. State of U.P.
key_held: Registration of FIR does not automatically warrant arrest. Arrest must be justified; mere suspicion is insufficient. Guidelines for arrest laid down.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)

case_name: State of Haryana v. Dinesh Kumar
key_held: Distinction between arrest and custody explained. Arrest is a specific act; custody is broader.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)

case_name: Delhi Judicial Services Association v. State of Gujarat
key_held: Guidelines for arrest of judicial officers: prior intimation to District Judge/High Court; no immediate police station visit without DSJ order.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)

case_name: Sheela Barse v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Magistrate must inform arrested person of right to medical examination under CrPC 54.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)

case_name: Vinubhai Haribhai Malviya v. State of Gujarat
key_held: Criminal investigation and inquiry must be guided by the 'hovering omnipresence of Article 21'. Fair process for accused and victim.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)

case_name: Ishwar Pratap Singh v. State of U.P.
key_held: Criminal investigations are the exclusive domain of police. Superior courts can monitor in exceptional cases but should not supervise the process.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)

case_name: Satender Kumar Antil v. CBI
key_held: Bail is the rule and jail is the exception. Detailed guidelines on when arrest is necessary. Discretion to arrest must be exercised sparingly.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)

case_name: Purkayastha v. State
key_held: Under UAPA and BNSS Sec 47, it is mandatory to inform the accused of specific 'grounds' of arrest along with right to seek bail.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)

case_name: Vernon Gonsalves v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Even under stringent laws like UAPA, courts must undertake meaningful exercise to see if alleged acts fall within the statute before denying bail.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)

case_name: Javed Gulam Nabi Sheikh v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: If State cannot ensure speedy trial, it cannot oppose bail solely on gravity of offence. Article 21 prevails.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)

case_name: Jalaluddin Khan v. Union of India
key_held: Reaffirmed 'bail is the rule and jail is the exception' even in serious cases.
chapter_name: Arrest, Police Powers & Guidelines (BNSS Sec 35 / CrPC 41)

case_name: Hussainara Khatoon v. Home Secretary, State of Bihar
key_held: Right to speedy trial is part of Article 21. Undertrials languishing in jail without trial for long periods must be released.
notes: Pioneer on speedy trial and undertrial rights.

case_name: Union of India v. K.A. Najeeb
key_held: Stringent bail provisions in special statutes cannot be used to incarcerate accused indefinitely when trial is unlikely to conclude soon.

case_name: Shabir Ahmed Shah v. National Investigation Agency
key_held: Prolonged detention where trial is unlikely to conclude in reasonable time results in undue curtailment of liberty under Art 21. Rigours of stringent laws melt down.
notes: 2026 ruling on undertrial rights under BNSS.

case_name: V. Senthil Balaji v. Deputy Director, Enforcement Directorate
key_held: Prolonged incarceration and slow trial can be grounds for bail even in stringent economic offences.

case_name: In Re: Inhuman Conditions in 1382 Prisons
key_held: Directed undertrial review committees, prison reforms, and liberal approach to bail for undertrials.

case_name: Bail is the rule, jail is the exception (multiple reiterations)
key_held: Repeatedly held that bail should be granted routinely except in cases involving serious offences with flight risk or tampering.

case_name: Satender Kumar Antil guidelines on default bail
key_held: Detailed framework for grant of default bail and when police can seek extension of remand.

case_name: Mukesh Kumar v. State (various)
key_held: Undertrials who have served half the maximum sentence entitled to bail (with exceptions).

case_name: Arnesh Kumar principles extended to bail
key_held: Courts must consider necessity of arrest before denying bail in <7 year offences.

case_name: Umar Khalid bail matters (Delhi Riots)
key_held: Delay in trial cannot be a 'trump card' for automatic bail in UAPA cases; but prolonged incarceration must be considered.

case_name: Sharjeel Imam & Vernon Gonsalves cases
key_held: Courts must examine whether alleged acts actually fall within stringent statutes before denying bail.

case_name: Javed Gulam Nabi Sheikh
key_held: State's inability to ensure speedy trial is a strong ground for bail regardless of offence gravity.

case_name: Default bail in multiple cases
key_held: BNSS provisions on first-time offenders (1/3rd) and general 1/2 rule clarified with exceptions for life/death offences.

case_name: PMLA bail conditions
key_held: Twin conditions for bail in PMLA are stringent but prolonged incarceration can still lead to release.

case_name: NALSA & undertrial review
key_held: Supreme Court directed NALSA and district committees to periodically review undertrial cases for release.

case_name: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab
key_held: Death penalty is constitutional but to be imposed only in the 'rarest of rare' cases. Individualized sentencing required; aggravating vs mitigating circumstances.
notes: Most cited death penalty case. 'Rarest of rare' doctrine.

case_name: Shatrughan Chauhan v. Union of India
key_held: Mental illness and inordinate delay in deciding mercy petitions are supervening circumstances for commuting death sentence.

case_name: Accused X v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Post-conviction mental illness is a valid mitigating factor for commuting death penalty to life imprisonment.

case_name: Md. Mannan @ Abdul Mannan v. State of Bihar
key_held: Severe mental illness at time of execution or strong possibility thereof can be ground for commutation.

case_name: Shabnam v. Union of India
key_held: Public opinion or 'collective conscience' cannot be used to impose death penalty. Courts must not become spokesmen of public opinion.

case_name: Santosh Kumar Satishbhushan Bariyar v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Public opinion has no role in death penalty adjudication. It is neither a circumstance of crime nor criminal.

case_name: Mukesh & Anr v. NCT of Delhi (Nirbhaya)
key_held: Confirmed death penalty in gangrape-murder. Emphasized rarest of rare and sentencing procedure.

case_name: Sudam @ Rahul v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Pendency of second mercy petition does not stay death warrant if first was rejected and not challenged.

case_name: Anil @ Anthony Arikswamy Joseph v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Death commuted to life without remission for 30 years. State must produce material on reformation possibility.

case_name: Jawed Khan v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Prison conduct, education in jail, and remorse are strong indicators of reformation for commuting death.

case_name: Dyaneshwar Suresh Borkar v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Graduation from jail, writing poems showing remorse, young age, and long incarceration are mitigating factors.

case_name: Bharat Singh v. State (NCT of Delhi)
key_held: Delhi HC (upheld principles) called for Probation Officer report + psychological assessment before death sentence.

case_name: Maru Ram v. Union of India
key_held: Life imprisonment means imprisonment for the whole of the remaining period of the convicted person's natural life.

case_name: Gopal Vinayak Godse v. State
key_held: Sentence of imprisonment for life must prima facie be treated as imprisonment for the whole of the remaining natural life.

case_name: In Re: Guidelines for sensitivity in sexual offence cases
key_held: Directed National Judicial Academy to frame guidelines for judges dealing with sexual offences and vulnerable victims.

case_name: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
key_held: Section 66A of IT Act struck down as unconstitutional (vague and overbroad). Violates Article 19(1)(a). Sections 69A and 79 upheld with safeguards.
chapter_name: Cyber Crimes, IT Act & Digital Evidence
notes: Landmark free speech judgment. All courts now refuse 66A prosecutions.

case_name: Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer
key_held: Electronic records as secondary evidence are admissible only if conditions of Section 65B of Evidence Act are satisfied.
chapter_name: Cyber Crimes, IT Act & Digital Evidence
notes: Mandatory for all digital evidence (CCTV, WhatsApp, emails).

case_name: Avnish Bajaj v. State (Bazee.com)
key_held: Intermediary liability issues in obscene material uploaded by users. Bail granted; principles on due diligence.
chapter_name: Cyber Crimes, IT Act & Digital Evidence

case_name: Syed Asifuddin v. State of A.P.
key_held: Tampering with mobile phone ESN amounts to tampering with computer source document under IT Act 65.
chapter_name: Cyber Crimes, IT Act & Digital Evidence

case_name: State of Tamil Nadu v. Suhas Katti
key_held: First conviction under Sec 67 IT Act for posting obscene/defamatory messages. Speedy trial (7 months).
chapter_name: Cyber Crimes, IT Act & Digital Evidence

case_name: Frios v. State of Kerala
key_held: Upheld validity of protected system notification under IT Act Section 70 for government software.
chapter_name: Cyber Crimes, IT Act & Digital Evidence

case_name: Poona Auto Ancillaries v. Punjab National Bank
key_held: Bank held negligent for phishing; Rs.45 lakh compensation awarded under IT Act 43A.
chapter_name: Cyber Crimes, IT Act & Digital Evidence

case_name: Harpal Singh v. State (various)
key_held: Strict compliance with Section 65B certificate for CCTV, call detail records, and electronic evidence.
chapter_name: Cyber Crimes, IT Act & Digital Evidence

case_name: R v. Gold & Schifreen (influential)
key_held: Mere unauthorized access without damage discussed in context of hacking laws.
chapter_name: Cyber Crimes, IT Act & Digital Evidence

case_name: Kumar v. Whiteley (JANET)
key_held: Unauthorized access and alteration of computer data amounts to hacking.
chapter_name: Cyber Crimes, IT Act & Digital Evidence

case_name: Kshipra Uke & Shiv Shankar Das v. State of Maharashtra (affirmed SC)
key_held: Intellectual property, research data, laptops and digital materials are 'property' under SC/ST POA Act. Theft/destruction = atrocity. Compensation payable.
notes: Landmark expansion of 'property'. SC upheld Bombay HC.

case_name: Ashabai v. State of Maharashtra (Full Bench Bombay, SC principles)
key_held: Complainant need not necessarily disclose caste of accused in the complaint for registration under SC/ST POA.

case_name: National Commission for SC directions enforcement
key_held: High Courts and lower courts directed to implement NCSC recommendations for compensation, SIT investigation in atrocity cases.

case_name: Exclusive Special Courts
key_held: States directed to establish Exclusive Special Courts under Section 14 SC/ST POA for speedy trial of atrocities.

case_name: No anticipatory bail under Sec 18
key_held: Section 18 SC/ST POA creates a bar on anticipatory bail. Courts follow strictly unless no prima facie offence.

case_name: Victim compensation & rights under 15A
key_held: State must provide comprehensive relief including travel, medical, legal aid, and compensation under SC/ST Rules.

case_name: Intellectual property as property (SC affirmation)
key_held: Supreme Court affirmed Bombay HC: intangible property (IP, research data) qualifies for relief under Atrocities Act.

case_name: Nandini Satpathy v. P.L. Dani
key_held: Article 20(3) (self-incrimination) and CrPC 161(2) (right to silence) cover substantially the same area.

case_name: Manoharan v. State
key_held: Section 164 CrPC does not mandate presence of lawyer for every confession except when recorded through audio-video means.

case_name: Somasundaram v. State
key_held: Statement recorded u/s 164 CrPC is not substantive evidence. Conviction cannot be based solely on it without corroboration.

case_name: Virender Singh v. State of Haryana
key_held: Statements u/s 161 CrPC are not substantive evidence and cannot be sole basis for conviction.

case_name: Tehsildar Singh v. State of U.P.
key_held: Significant omissions from police statement can amount to contradiction if deemed part of the statement.

case_name: Harbir Singh v. Sheeshpal
key_held: Delay in recording witness statements does not automatically discredit testimony if cogent and satisfactorily explained.

case_name: Raghunandan v. State of U.P.
key_held: Judge's power under Evidence Act 165 to question witnesses overrides restrictions of CrPC 162.

case_name: State of Maharashtra v. M.H. George
key_held: Mens rea is presumed necessary for statutory offences unless statute clearly indicates otherwise.

case_name: KM Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: General burden of proving every element of offence remains on the prosecution.

case_name: Dayabhai Thakkar v. State of Gujarat
key_held: For insanity defense, accused must prove by preponderance that he was incapable of knowing nature/wrongfulness of act.

case_name: Bimbadhar Pradhan v. State of Orissa
key_held: Individual can be convicted of criminal conspiracy even if co-conspirators are acquitted.

case_name: State of Tamil Nadu v. Nalini (Rajiv Gandhi case)
key_held: Mere association or knowledge is not enough for conspiracy. Actual agreement (meeting of minds) is sine qua non.

case_name: State (NCT of Delhi) v. Shiv Charan Bansal
key_held: Criminal conspiracy is typically proved by cumulative effect of circumstantial evidence.

case_name: Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India
key_held: Orders under Section 144 CrPC have direct impact on fundamental rights. Must balance with proportionality; apply less intrusive measures.

case_name: Anita Thakur v. State of J&K
key_held: Right to peaceful protest is a fundamental right. Police force only warranted when assembly turns violent.

case_name: Mohd. Ahmad Khan v. Shah Bano Begum
key_held: Divorced Muslim women entitled to maintenance under CrPC 125 if unable to maintain themselves (beyond iddat).

case_name: D. Velusamy v. D. Patchaiammal
key_held: Laid down criteria to determine if a live-in relationship qualifies as 'relationship in the nature of marriage' for maintenance.

case_name: Nanak Chandra v. Chandra Kishore
key_held: Maintenance provisions under CrPC 125-128 are secular and apply to all persons irrespective of religion.

case_name: Yamunabai v. Anantrao
key_held: Term 'wife' in CrPC 125 refers only to a legally wedded wife. Second wife whose marriage is void cannot claim.

case_name: Dr. Swapan Kumar Banerjee v. State of West Bengal
key_held: Wife divorced on grounds of desertion is still entitled to maintenance under CrPC 125.

case_name: Sanjeev Kapoor v. Chandana Kapoor
key_held: Court does not become functus officio after passing maintenance order under CrPC 125. Can alter under Sec 127.

case_name: Vijay Manohar Arbat v. Kashi Rao Raja Ram
key_held: Daughter (married or not) is legally obligated to pay maintenance to parents if unable to maintain themselves.

case_name: In Re: Summoning of Advocates
key_held: Investigating officers cannot summon advocates representing accused to provide case details (with limited exceptions).
